title: Hardwood Promises High-Speed JVM Apache Parquet Processing with Zero Mandatory Dependencies
gist: Hardwood, a Gunnar Morling-initiated open-source JVM library, offers zero-mandatory-dependency, multi-threaded Apache Parquet reading that hits 16.5M rows/sec on 8 vCPUs, targeting the dependency bloat and single-threaded bottleneck of the traditional Parquet Java implementation.

insights.claim: Hardwood spreads Parquet page decoding across all available CPU cores rather than processing sequentially, and in flat-dataset scans on 8 vCPUs achieved 16.5 million rows per second throughput.
insights.speaker: Olimpiu Pop
insights.so_what: Concrete multi-core throughput number gives data engineers a benchmark to justify migrating off the single-threaded Parquet Java reader.
insights.locator: Benchmark results indicate that Hardwood achieves significant throughput

insights.claim: Hardwood targets the traditional Apache Parquet Java implementation's two core weaknesses: significant dependency overhead and a single-threaded core reader that constrains performance.
insights.speaker: Olimpiu Pop
insights.so_what: Names the specific incumbent pain points a new entrant is exploiting — useful for gauging displacement potential in JVM data tooling.

insights.claim: Hardwood implements branchless, batch-at-a-time predicate evaluation during filtered scans to minimize CPU branch mispredictions, a critical performance factor in modern analytical data processing.
insights.speaker: Olimpiu Pop
insights.so_what: Low-level CPU optimizations signal a serious performance-engineering focus, not a toy project.
insights.locator: It also implements optimised predicate evaluation

insights.claim: Hardwood ships with a zero-mandatory-dependency profile using Java 9's minimal logging abstraction to avoid external logging dependencies, explicitly to reduce supply-chain attack risk and classpath conflicts, with LZ4/GZip compression and S3 support offered as optional pull-in dependencies.
insights.speaker: Olimpiu Pop
insights.so_what: Supply-chain risk reduction is now a first-class selling point for infrastructure libraries — a differentiator enterprises increasingly weigh.
insights.locator: The library is designed with a zero-mandatory-dependency profile

insights.claim: Hardwood progressed from inception in early 2026 to a stable 1.0 release in just five months, currently offering read capabilities with write support on the near-term roadmap.
insights.speaker: Olimpiu Pop
insights.so_what: Rapid maturation plus missing write support means it is read-only today — a gating factor for full adoption.
insights.locator: Five months since its inception in early 2026

insights.claim: Beyond Gunnar Morling, the project attracted 20 open-source contributors including veteran Java-space contributors Andres Almiray and Bruno Borges, with community feedback mostly positive.
insights.speaker: Olimpiu Pop
insights.so_what: Involvement of established Java-community figures raises the odds of sustained maintenance and adoption.

insights.claim: Hardwood was built using AI-assisted coding while keeping design and code review under human ownership, a signal of how AI-augmented development is producing production infrastructure libraries.
insights.speaker: Olimpiu Pop
insights.so_what: A concrete example of AI-assisted coding reaching stable open-source infra — a data point on AI's real productivity impact.
insights.quote: The project utilised AI-assisted coding during development, though the design and code review processes remained under human ownership.
